ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Cucur Udang / Prawn Fritters. The broader fritter class (FOODON_03315091) is used as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific term for Southeast Asian prawn fritters is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters — battered and deep-fried dough products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers battered/fried dough products broadly. No specific code for prawn fritters exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue; broader category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Cucur Udang or prawn fritters as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Cucur Udang is approximately 100 g, equivalent to 3–4 medium-sized prawn fritters, as commonly sold at Malaysian and Singaporean street food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat; Wheat flour, all-purpose) for micronutrient cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Cucur Udang exists in the HPB Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from the HPB Singapore database entry for deep-fried prawn snacks and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and all-purpose wheat flour (FDC ID 169761). Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are consistent with the composite ingredient profile. Input record provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.9; values are retained where consistent with approved-source analogues and flagged in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried wheat-flour snack (GI ~65)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cucur Udang. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy with battered deep-fried wheat-flour snacks from the University of Sydney GI Database. The refined wheat flour base has a high GI (~70–75), moderated downward by the protein content from prawns and egg, fat from deep-frying, and dietary fibre from bean sprouts and chives, yielding a composite estimate of GI 65. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 24.5) / 100 = 15.9 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) and elevated total fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Cucur Udang intake to occasional small portions, as the 380 mg sodium per 100 g contributes approximately 17% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the Singapore HPB, and the high fat load from deep-frying may further elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65) and moderate carbohydrate load (24.5 g per 100 g) from refined wheat flour batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Cucur Udang in small portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables or a protein source to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the refined wheat flour batter contributes a medium GI of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 15.9 per 100 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 12.8 g per 100 g including saturated fat from deep-frying, and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Cucur Udang as an occasional indulgence, limiting portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, and preferring versions fried in oils with a more favourable unsaturated fatty acid profile such as sunflower or canola oil.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Contains whole prawns (crustacean shellfish) as a primary ingredient
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Cucur Udang is contraindicated for individuals with a crustacean shellfish allergy, as whole prawns are embedded throughout the fritter and cannot be removed without cross-contamination.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cucur Udang, known in English as Prawn Fritters, is a popular Malay savory deep-fried snack widely sold at street food stalls and 'gorengan' (fried food) carts throughout Malaysia and Singapore. The fritter is made by embedding small to medium whole prawns — with or without shells — into a thick seasoned batter of wheat flour, eggs, and water. The batter is enriched with bean sprouts (taugeh), chives (kucai), and sliced onions for textural contrast and fresh flavour, and coloured a vibrant golden-yellow with turmeric powder. The fritters are deep-fried until the exterior is airy and crispy while the prawns inside remain tender and juicy. They are traditionally served hot alongside spicy peanut sauce or sweet chili dipping sauce. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 245 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 12.8 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate, with 380 mg sodium — making it a moderately energy-dense, higher-sodium snack. The medium glycemic index of approximately 65 reflects the refined wheat flour base, partially offset by protein, fat, and fibre from the composite ingredients. Cucur Udang is a favourite tea-time snack and breakfast item, and is culturally significant as an everyday street food in the Malay culinary tradition. Clinically, it is best consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, fat, and refined carbohydrate content. It is not suitable for those with crustacean shellfish or gluten allergies.

What is the GI of prawn fritters?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Cucur Udang exists in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from approved-source analogues (HPB deep-fried prawn snack entries and USDA SR Legacy shrimp and wheat flour entries) and are consistent with the ai_generated input record (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat is estimated from the composite ingredient profile. Confidence is therefore rated medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Cucur Udang as a composite deep-fried dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. Saturated fat (2.1 g) is estimated from the composite ingredient profile using USDA SR Legacy values for vegetable frying oil and shrimp. 2. No core macro (protein_g 8.5, fat_g 12.8, carbohydrate_g 24.5) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. 3. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on battered deep-fried wheat-flour snacks; no direct GI measurement for Cucur Udang is available in the published literature as of 2024. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 24.5) / 100 = 15.9 g per 100 g reference serving. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315091 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are used as broader-category anchors; no exact ontology code for Cucur Udang exists in current releases. 6. Input record provenance is ai_generated; all values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained where consistent.
